name: Pumpkin Cranberry Yeast Bread

ingredients.item: • 1 cup plumped golden or black raisins (soaked in warm
• 2/3 cup fresh cranberries, thawed and patted dry

method: In a large mixing basin, whisk together 2 cups flour, cinnamon,
nutmeg, and salt until well combined. Remove from the
equation.
In a small mixing basin, combine the warm water, yeast, and
stir. Allow 5 minutes for the yeast to bloom.
Cream the butter and sugar together in a mixer equipped with
the paddle attachment on medium speed. Next, mix in the
pumpkin puree and the egg until everything is well combined.
The mixture may appear curdled at this stage, but don't worry;
after adding the dry ingredients, it will come together.
Reduce the mixer speed to low and add the yeast, followed by
1/2 cup at a flour mixture. Transfer to the dough hook as soon as
the ingredients begin to form a dough that holds together. Knead
the dough for a few minutes. If your dough isn't coming
together, add a tablespoon of flour at a time until it does. The
dough will be pretty wet.
Mix the dough for 10 minutes at low speed, scraping the sides of
the basin and the hook with a rubber spatula as needed. The
dough will first resemble batter rather than dough, but it will
transform into a soft, sticky dough that will ball up on the hook
as you work with it. (This dough develops similarly to brioche
dough.)
Add the pecans and drained raisins to the machine on low speed
and mix only until mixed, about 1 minute. To prevent crushing
the cranberries, add them last and mix as little as possible.
(Some of the cranberries will inevitably explode, staining a
piece of the dough crimson.) Just think of it as adorable, and
you'll be OK.)
First Rise: Scrape the dough into a lightly buttered large mixing
bowl, cover firmly with plastic wrap, and leave away at room
temperature to rise for approximately 2 hours, or until about
doubled in volume.
Shaping the dough: Lightly grease or spray two 8 x 4′′ bread
loaf pans (or, for a large loaf, one 9 x 5 loaf pan) or retain the
dough in a round to bake as a freeform round.
Divide the dough into two parts and pat each piece into a 5 x 7′′
rectangle on a lightly floured board. Start rolling the dough
jelly-roll style from the long side of each rectangle and seal the
seam by pushing it together with your fingertips. Seal the ends,
then lay each roll seam side down in a loaf pan that has been
prepped. Any revealed fruit on the top of the loaf should be
carefully pushed into the dough so it is covered; otherwise, it
will burn and be bitter while cooking. (To make a freeform
round, roll the dough into a ball and lay it on parchment paper.)
Second Rise: Cover the pans (or circle) with a
kitchen towel and set them aside to rise for 1 1/2 to 2 hours at
room temperature or until the dough has almost doubled in size.
It will increase to just above the pan rims (mine barely got there
did just rise above the height of the pan in the centre).
Set the oven to 350° F and centre a rack in the
oven. Preheat the oven to 350°F and bake the loaves for 35
minutes, or until golden brown. Place the pans on a cooling rack
to cool. Allow for 5 minutes of cooking time before turning the
loaves out of their pans and cooling on a rack to room
temperature.
Conclusion
Bread is made by baking flour, water, salt, yeast, and other components
combination. The fundamental procedure combines the ingredients until
they form a firm paste or dough, then baking the dough into a loaf.
The goals of the bread-making methods employed in New Zealand
(mechanical dough development, bulk fermentation, and no-time doughs)
create a dough that rises readily and has the characteristics necessary to
make excellent bread for consumers.
To produce excellent bread, every method of dough preparation must be
flexible enough to relax and expand during rising. When you tug on a good
dough, it should spread out. It must also be elastic, meaning it must be
strong enough to retain the gases generated as it rises and stable enough to
maintain its form and cell structure.
When two proteins found in flour (gliadin and glutenin) are combined with
water, they create gluten. Gluten is what gives dough its unique
characteristics. Gluten is required to produce bread and impacts the
mixing, kneading, and baking parts of dough. It's crucial to understand
